Visualize wGRR plasmid networks

Misha

2026-03-10

library(dplyr)
## 
## Attaching package: 'dplyr'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter, lag
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
library(readr)
library(stringr)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.5     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
## ✔ purrr     1.2.1
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
dist_mat <- read.csv('../output/xanthomonas_wGRR_distance_matrix.csv', row.names = 1)
dist_obj <- as.dist(dist_mat)

Visualizing a distance matrix can be done in many ways. Here we explore a dimensionality reduction method (MDS) to visualize the plasmids in 2D space (somewhat similar to PCA):

mds_result <- cmdscale(
  d   = dist_obj,
  k   = 2,
  eig = TRUE,
  add = TRUE
  )

Just like a PCA we can find the variance explained by the two axes.

variance_explained <- mds_result$eig / sum(mds_result$eig[mds_result$eig > 0]) * 100
cat(round(variance_explained[1:2], 1), "%")
## 2.6 1.7 %

Oof that’s very low! Keep that in mind while looking at the following scatter plots.

To plot a scatter plot first we get the coordinates from the mds_results

coords <- as.data.frame(mds_result$points)
colnames(coords) <- paste0("MDS", seq_len(2))
coords$plasmid_id <- rownames(dist_mat)
coords <- coords[, c("plasmid_id", paste0("MDS", seq_len(2)))]

Plot plasmids in MDS space:

coords %>% ggplot(aes(x = MDS1, y = MDS2)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.5) + theme_bw()

metadata <- read_tsv('../data/xanthomonas_metadata.tsv', col_names = c("accession", "species", "taxid", "length"))
## Rows: 479 Columns: 4
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: "\t"
## chr (2): accession, species
## dbl (2): taxid, length
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
metadata <- metadata %>% mutate(species_clean = stringr::word(species, 1, 2))

Merge metadata with the plasmid coordinates, plot with color for species:

coords <- merge(coords, metadata, by.x = "plasmid_id", by.y = "accession")
coords %>% ggplot(aes(x = MDS1, y = MDS2, colour = species_clean)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.5) + theme_bw()

This is quite cool! It shows different clusters of plasmids, with different colors within clusters. This could indicate plasmid (parts) being shared between different species, however, we need to keep in mind the low percentage of variance explained with the MDA.

coords %>% filter(MDS1 > 0.5) %>% filter(MDS2 > 0) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = MDS1, y = MDS2, colour = species_clean, size = length)) + 
  geom_point(alpha = 1) + 
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Paired") +
  theme_void()

library(gghighlight)

coords %>% ggplot(aes(x = MDS1, y = MDS2, colour = species_clean)) + 
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) + 
  gghighlight() +
  facet_wrap(~ species_clean) + 
  theme_void() 
## label_key: species_clean
## Too many data points, skip labeling

I want to find plasmid pairs of plasmids from different species, and then look for high wGRR values

metadata_minimal <- metadata %>% select(accession, species_clean)

wgrr_pairs <- dist_mat %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("pA") %>%
  pivot_longer(-1, names_to = "pB", values_to = "distance") %>%
  rename(pA = 1) %>% 
  mutate(wGRR = 1 - distance) %>% 
  filter(pA != pB) %>%        # exclude self
  filter(pA < pB)         # deduplicate if symmetric


wgrr_pairs_species <- wgrr_pairs %>% 
  merge(metadata_minimal, by.x = "pA", by.y = "accession") %>% 
  mutate(species_pA = species_clean) %>% 
  select(-species_clean) %>% 
  merge(metadata_minimal, by.x = "pB", by.y = "accession") %>% 
  mutate(species_pB = species_clean) %>% 
  select(-species_clean) %>% 
  mutate(species_pair = paste(species_pA, species_pB, sep = "-"))
wgrr_pairs_species %>% group_by(species_pair) %>% 
  summarise(mean = mean(wGRR),
            median = median(wGRR), 
            min = min(wGRR),
            max = max(wGRR))
## # A tibble: 450 × 5
##    species_pair                                        mean median    min    max
##    <chr>                                              <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>
##  1 Xanthomonas albilineans-Xanthomonas albilineans   0.236  0.119  0.0594 0.868 
##  2 Xanthomonas albilineans-Xanthomonas arboricola    0.0558 0.0502 0      0.138 
##  3 Xanthomonas albilineans-Xanthomonas axonopodis    0.0391 0.0217 0      0.150 
##  4 Xanthomonas albilineans-Xanthomonas campestris    0.0504 0.0211 0      0.718 
##  5 Xanthomonas albilineans-Xanthomonas citri         0.0452 0.0348 0      0.517 
##  6 Xanthomonas albilineans-Xanthomonas cucurbitae    0.0482 0      0      0.142 
##  7 Xanthomonas albilineans-Xanthomonas dyei          0      0      0      0     
##  8 Xanthomonas albilineans-Xanthomonas euroxanthea   0.398  0.398  0.0501 0.745 
##  9 Xanthomonas albilineans-Xanthomonas euvesicatoria 0.0319 0.0217 0      0.0918
## 10 Xanthomonas albilineans-Xanthomonas fragariae     0.112  0.0482 0      0.700 
## # ℹ 440 more rows
wgrr_pairs_species %>% 
  filter(species_pA != species_pB) %>% 
  filter(wGRR > 0.95)
##                pB            pA     distance      wGRR

## 103 Xanthomonas euvesicatoria-Xanthomonas perforans
## 104          Xanthomonas citri-Xanthomonas hortorum
## 105          Xanthomonas citri-Xanthomonas hortorum
## 106          Xanthomonas citri-Xanthomonas hortorum
## 107          Xanthomonas citri-Xanthomonas hortorum
## 108          Xanthomonas citri-Xanthomonas hortorum
## 109      Xanthomonas perforans-Xanthomonas hortorum

The final one is a really interesting result. It should cross-species plasmid pairs with very high wGRR values.

library(ggraph)
library(tidygraph)
## 
## Attaching package: 'tidygraph'
## The following object is masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter

Alternative visualization in 2D space is a network (see Eduardo Rocha’s papers). In these networks each plasmid becomes a node (a dot) and each wGRR connection (based on a threshold) is an edge (a line). It makes sense to only consider edges representing a certain level of similarity, e.g. wGRR > 0.5.

edges <- wgrr_pairs_species %>%
  filter(wGRR >= 0.3) %>%
  select(pA, pB, wGRR, species_pA, species_pB)

# Node table with species annotation
nodes <- data.frame(name = unique(c(edges$pA, edges$pB))) %>%
  left_join(
    wgrr_pairs_species %>% select(pA, species_pA) %>% distinct() %>% rename(name = pA, species = species_pA),
    by = "name"
  )

g <- tbl_graph(nodes = nodes, edges = edges, directed = FALSE, node_key = "name")

Draw the network:

ggraph(g, layout = "fr") +  # Fruchterman-Reingold, good for clusters
  geom_edge_link(aes(alpha = wGRR), colour = "grey60") +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = species), size = 1) +
  # geom_node_text(aes(label = name), repel = TRUE, size = 2.5) +
  #scale_edge_width(range = c(0.3, 2)) +
  scale_edge_alpha(range = c(0.3, 0.9)) +
  #scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_graph() +
  labs(title = "Plasmid relatedness network (wGRR ≥ 0.4)",
       colour = "Host species", edge_width = "wGRR") + theme_void()
## Ignoring unknown labels:
## • edge_width : "wGRR"

Highlight cross-species edges in the network:

g <- g %>%
  activate(edges) %>%
  mutate(cross_species = species_pA != species_pB)

ggraph(g, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(colour = cross_species, width = wGRR, alpha = wGRR)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = species), size = 2) +
  scale_edge_colour_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "grey80", "TRUE" = "firebrick")) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.3, 2)) +
  scale_edge_alpha(range = c(0.2, 0.9)) +
  theme_graph() +
  labs(title = "Cross-species plasmid transfer network",
       edge_colour = "Cross-species")

Repeat but only include cross species interactions in building the network in the first place

# keep only cross-species edges
edges_cross <- wgrr_pairs_species %>%
  filter(wGRR >= 0.8, species_pA != species_pB) %>%
  select(pA, pB, wGRR, species_pA, species_pB)

# nodes automatically limited to only those in cross-species edges
nodes_cross <- data.frame(name = unique(c(edges_cross$pA, edges_cross$pB))) %>%
  left_join(
    wgrr_pairs_species %>% select(pA, species_pA) %>% distinct() %>% rename(name = pA, species = species_pA),
    by = "name"
  )

g_cross <- tbl_graph(nodes = nodes_cross, edges = edges_cross, directed = FALSE, node_key = "name")
ggraph(g_cross, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(colour = "firebrick") +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = species), size = 1) +
  # scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_graph() +
  labs(title = "Cross-species plasmid transfer network (wGRR ≥ 0.8)",
       colour = "Host species")

Pretty cool!

Each sub graph thats not connected to others is called a ‘component’. We can add that information and then pull out the particular accession names in each component.

# add component membership
g_cross <- g_cross %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  mutate(component = group_components())

# summarise each component: size, n species, mean wGRR
component_summary <- g_cross %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  group_by(component) %>%
  summarise(
    n_plasmids = n(),
    n_species = n_distinct(species),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(n_species), desc(n_plasmids))

I’m curious about component 8 in this case, which contains 5 plasmids from 4 different species!

target_component <- 2  # replace with your component of interest

plasmids_of_interest <- g_cross %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  filter(component == target_component) 

cat(plasmids_of_interest %>% pull(name))
## NC_003922.1 NC_003921.3 NC_020801.1 NC_020797.1 NZ_CP008988.1 NZ_CP018860.1 NZ_CP009021.1 NZ_CP009003.1 NZ_CP060001.1 NZ_CP018852.1 NZ_CP024033.1 NZ_CP008991.1 NZ_CP009024.1 NZ_CP006856.1 NZ_CP018849.1 NZ_CP059993.1 NZ_CP009000.1 NZ_CP009012.1 NZ_CP009027.1 NZ_CP024030.1 NZ_CP009006.1 NZ_CP009015.1 NZ_CP009009.1 NZ_CP008997.1 NZ_CP013664.1 NZ_CP008994.1 NZ_CP009018.1 NZ_CP018856.1 NZ_CP060000.1 NZ_CP060003.1 NZ_CP023287.1 NZ_CP009014.1 NZ_CP008990.1 NZ_CP008999.1 NZ_CP008996.1 NZ_CP009008.1 NZ_CP006855.1 NZ_CP009011.1 NZ_CP009020.1 NZ_CP009005.1 NZ_CP013665.1 NZ_CP024031.1 NZ_CP008993.1 NZ_CP009023.1 NZ_CP009026.1 NZ_CP024032.1 NZ_CP008987.1 NZ_CP009002.1 NZ_CP009017.1 NZ_CP062256.2 NZ_CP062257.2 NZ_CP066347.1 NZ_CP075327.1 NZ_CP075329.1 NZ_CP096228.1 NZ_CP096229.1

Final visualization option: UMAP

library(uwot)
## Loading required package: Matrix
## 
## Attaching package: 'Matrix'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:tidyr':
## 
##     expand, pack, unpack
library(ggplot2)

dist_mat_object <- dist(dist_mat)

# run UMAP on the distance matrix
umap_res <- umap(dist_mat, n_neighbors = 10, min_dist = 2)

# build a plottable dataframe
umap_df <- data.frame(
  UMAP1 = umap_res[, 1],
  UMAP2 = umap_res[, 2],
  plasmid = rownames(dist_mat)
) %>%
  left_join(
    wgrr_pairs_species %>% select(pA, species_pA) %>% distinct() %>% rename(plasmid = pA, species = species_pA),
    by = "plasmid"
  )

ggplot(umap_df, aes(UMAP1, UMAP2, colour = species)) +
  geom_point(size = 1.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(colour = "Host species")
